Document [0] (Title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Hormone Therapy) Initial therapy with leuprolide, goserelin, and similar luteinizing hormone–releasing hormone (LHRH) agonists should be preceded with anti-androgen therapy, such as bicalutamide, when the PSA level is greater than 10 ng/mL to prevent any clinical response to the temporary testosterone surge that typically accompanies the initiation of hormonal therapy with these agents. Document [2] (Title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Hormone Therapy) Relugolix is an oral LHRH antagonist that is now FDA-approved and available. Similar to injectable degarelix, relugolix is a direct LHRH antagonist and causes a very rapid decrease in serum testosterone. Relugolix is quite effective as it has been shown to sustain castrate levels of testosterone in 97% of men tested (compared to 89% of men treated with leuprolide) and appears to have fewer major cardiovascular events. [241] Similar to degarelix, relugolix is approved for prostate cancer that is locally advanced, castrate-resistant, or metastatic, and for patients with biochemical recurrences. [241]

Document [4] (Title: Leuprolide -- Indications) In advanced prostate cancer, leuprolide is used as a palliative treatment to help reduce comorbidities and improve quality of life. Leuprolide lowers testosterone levels, which in turn minimizes the stimulatory effect it normally has on androgen-sensitive prostate cancer cells, therefore causing them to undergo apoptosis. [4]
Document [5] (Title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Hormone Therapy) Hormonal therapy has been found to improve survival when combined with radiation therapy but not with radical prostatectomy for intermediate (Gleason 3+4=7) and higher-grade disease. A common plan is to start with leuprolide or similar agents and monitor the PSA level monthly until it becomes undetectable or nadirs, at which time definitive radiation therapy (cyberknife, external beam, and brachytherapy seed implants) can be started. The hormonal therapy is typically continued for at least 1 year and optimally for at least 2 years after radiation. Intermittent hormone therapy is another option in selected cases to minimize the adverse effects of sustained, very low testosterone levels. Castration levels of testosterone have historically been considered <50 ng/dL, but newer data suggest that optimal results are obtained when testosterone levels are maintained at less than 20 ng/dL.

Document [8] (Title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Conservative Palliative Strategy) Often termed "watchful waiting," this modality is most commonly offered to elderly, frail, asymptomatic men. It is anticipated that disease progression in the remaining lifetime of the man is unlikely to cause a clinically significant problem. In addition, the morbidity/ mortality associated with radical treatment does not justify the anticipated benefits of longevity. Treatment in the form of primary androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) may be offered to control symptoms as and when they occur or if there is evidence of metastatic disease. The aim is remission, which occurs in around 80% of cases, rather than "cure." This approach has been called into question due to improvements in life expectancy, treatments, and imaging modalities. Additionally, evidence demonstrates significantly improved survival when radiotherapy is added to ADT for patients who previously would have received ADT alone. [36]
Document [9] (Title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Hormone Therapy) In 1941, Dr. Charles Huggins, a urologist from the University of Chicago, discovered that androgen deprivation (castration) could cause prostate glands to atrophy and lead to the regression of prostate cancer. [237] [238] [239] [240] He was awarded the Nobel Prize for Medicine in 1966 for his discovery, which is the basis for all hormonal (testosterone deprivation-based) treatments used in prostate cancer. The discovery was the first effective systemic therapy for prostate cancer, and it is still useful in inducing remission. This beneficial hormonal effect typically lasts an average of about 2 years, but virtually all prostate cancers eventually escape and regrow. Although bilateral orchiectomy was originally used to produce castration levels of testosterone, current hormonal therapy is typically administered with injectable medications.

Document [17] (Title: Fertility Sparing Management in Uterine Fibroids -- Treatment / Management -- Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Agonists) Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) agonists are peptide analogs of the GnRH molecule that act as agonists at the GnRH receptor in the pituitary gland.  Their initial activation at the GnRH receptor causes a flare effect, temporarily increasing serum gonadotropins and downstream sex hormone concentrations. Prolonged activation of the GnRH receptor, typically following 2-4 weeks of continuous therapy, leads to involution or downregulation of the receptor, thereby suppressing pituitary function leading to hypoestrogenism. [40] GnRH agonists have a diverse array of clinical applications. Regarding symptomatic fibroid management, they are used for short-term treatment of AUB, typically inducing a state of amenorrhea, and help improve symptoms of uterine enlargement. Given their side effect profile with long-term use, GnRH agonists are meant to be utilized as a bridge therapy prior to surgical or interventional procedures or natural menopause. [2] Leuprolide acetate, the most studied and most frequently administered GnRH agonist for fibroid therapy, is given as an intramuscular depot injection, either as a monthly injection of 3.75 mg for up to three months or an 11.25 mg 3-month formulation as a single dose.

Leuprolide discontinuation should merit consideration when at the appropriate time for puberty. [6]

Document [23] (Title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Summary of Prostate Cancer Systemic Therapy) All protocols begin with hormonal therapy using an LHRH agonist or antagonist. Due to improved outcomes and survival, a second-generation antiandrogen should be added to ADT in the upfront setting for the majority of patients. In advanced or aggressive cases, triplet therapy with docetaxel and a second-generation antiandrogen should be used. Docetaxel is the recommended first-line chemotherapy. Cabazitaxel is a second-line chemotherapy drug. Although similar in efficacy to docetaxel, cabazitaxel  is better tolerated and preferred in patients at risk for neutropenia, extremely frail patients, or older patients. Levonorgestrel is chemically derived from norgestrel, as indicated by its parent‑child relationship, and it is one of the active components contained in the combination product ethinyl estradiol/folic acid/levonorgestrel. Clinical guidance specifies that levonorgestrel is contraindicated in patients with ورم بطانة الرحم (uterine lining tumor), meaning it should not be used in that condition. In contrast, leuprolid, another member of L [Preparations], may be employed in the treatment of ورم بطانة الرحم. This juxtaposition underscores the distinct therapeutic roles and safety profiles of the two drugs within the same preparation family.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 431) L [Preparations] is a product family that includes the progestin levonorgestrel and the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone agonist leuprolid. Levonorgestrel, which is chemically derived from its parent compound norgestrel, serves as the active hormonal component in several combined oral contraceptive tablets: ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg / levonorgestrel 0.1 mg Oral Tablet, ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg / levonorgestrel 0.05 mg Oral Tablet, and ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg / levonorgestrel 0.15 mg Oral Tablet. In addition, levonorgestrel is present in a multi‑ingredient product that combines ethinyl estradiol, folic acid, and levonorgestrel. All of these tablets share polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive excipient. Clinical use of levonorgestrel is contraindicated in patients with ورم بطانة الرحم (uterine/endometrial cancer), reflecting the need to avoid progestogenic stimulation in this condition. Conversely, leuprolid, another member of the L [Preparations] family, may be employed as a therapeutic agent for ورم بطانة الرحم, illustrating its role in hormone‑dependent tumor management. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians select appropriate hormonal therapies while respecting contraindications and considering alternative treatment options.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2648) Progestogens and estrogens, fixed combinations are a specific type of progestogens and estrogens in combination, representing a subclass of combined hormone therapy in which the progestogen and estrogen components are formulated together in predetermined, unchanging doses.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1140) Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin that binds to the human progesterone receptor and is a key active ingredient in several combined oral contraceptive tablets, including ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg/levonorgestrel 0.1 mg, ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.05 mg, and ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.15 mg formulations. These tablets also contain the inactive excipient polyethylene glycol 400, and a broader combination product listed as ethiny l estradiol/folic acid/levonorgestrel likewise includes levonorgestrel. Chemically, levonorgestrel is derived from its parent compound norgestrel. Clinical use of levonorgestrel is contraindicated in patients with uterine lining tumors (endometrial carcinoma), reflecting the need for caution in this population. The category “L [Preparations]” encompasses formulations that contain levonorgestrel as well as the GnRH agonist leuprolide (referred to as leuprolid), indicating a shared product lineage. Leuprolide is employed in the therapeutic management of uterine lining tumors, highlighting its role in treating conditions for which levonorgestrel is contraindicated. Together, these relationships illustrate how levonorgestrel’s pharmacologic action, formulation components, metabolic ancestry, and safety considerations are interconnected within reproductive and oncologic care.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 63) Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin that belongs to the class of hormonal contraceptive agents and is included in the broader category of female contraceptive drugs. It is a key active ingredient in several combined oral tablets that also contain ethinyl estradiol, such as the 0.02 mg/0.1 mg, 0.03 mg/0.05 mg, and 0.03 mg/0.15 mg formulations. These tablets use polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive excipient. Human progesterone receptors are a pharmacologic target of levonorgestrel, mediating its contraceptive effects. Because levonorgestrel can stimulate progesterone‑responsive tissues, it is contraindicated in patients with uterine (endometrial) cancer. Leuprolide, another compound listed under the same “L [Preparations]” grouping, is a gonadotropin‑releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that may be employed in the treatment of uterine cancer, highlighting a therapeutic role that contrasts with the contraindication of levonorgestrel in the same disease. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is also classified as a hormonal and female contraceptive agent. Like levonorgestrel, it acts on the human progesterone receptor, providing progestogenic activity for contraception and other hormonal indications. Together, these relationships illustrate how levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate function as progesterone‑receptor‑targeting contraceptives, how levonorgestrel is formulated in specific oral tablets with ethinyl estradiol and polyethylene glycol 400, and how the safety profile of levonorgestrel requires avoidance in uterine cancer, whereas leuprolide may be used therapeutically for that condition. This integrated knowledge supports informed prescribing, patient counseling, and risk management in reproductive and oncologic care.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 233) (اضطراب الكلية) and in those with uterine lining tumors (ورم بطانة الرحم), reflecting safety concerns in these conditions. Conversely, the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone antagonist leuprolide (listed under the “L [Preparations]” category) may be employed in the treatment of uterine lining tumors, illustrating a therapeutic relationship opposite to the contraindication for levonorgestrel. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is another synthetic progestin that belongs to the same hormonal contraceptive class as levonorgestrel. It also targets the human progesterone receptor, underscoring a shared mechanism of action among progestin‑based contraceptives. Both levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate are classified under hormonal contraceptive substances in various linguistic contexts (e.g., Czech “hormonální antikoncepční látky” and “antikoncepční látky ženské”). Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154) Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) Agonist is classified as a hormone‑related agent and belongs to the leuprolide family of compounds. Within this family, specific agents such as triptorelin are recognized as members of the LHRH Agonist group. The broader category of L [Preparations] serves as the parent compound from which leuprolide derivatives are derived. Clinically, leuprolide and its related LHRH agonists are employed in the therapeutic management of uterine endometrial tumors (endometrial carcinoma), highlighting their role in endocrine‑driven cancer treatment.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 427) Androgens and estrogens are classified as a specific type of “androgens and female sex hormones in combination,” and they are also listed as a member of the broader grouping “estrogens / testosterone.” A more complex formulation, “androgens and female sex hormones in combination with other drugs,” is a specialized subclass of the same “androgens and female sex hormones in combination” category. Both of these combination categories fall under the higher‑level class “SEX HORMONES AND MODULATORS OF THE GENITAL SYSTEM,” which also includes “gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants.” This hierarchical organization links individual hormone combinations and their extended formulations to the overarching therapeutic class of sex hormones and genital system modulators, clarifying how specific agents relate to broader pharmacologic groups used in reproductive and endocrine care.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 28) Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestogen that serves as the active ingredient in several combined oral contraceptive tablets with ethinyl estradiol (ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg/levonorgestrel 0.1 mg, ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.05 mg, and ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.15 mg). In these formulations, polyethylene glycol 400 is used as an inactive excipient that functions as a filler or solvent, influencing tablet physical properties without pharmacologic activity and potentially affecting patients with excipient sensitivities. Levonorgestrel binds the human progesterone receptor, a molecular target it shares with the progestin medroxyprogesterone acetate. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is also classified as a hormonal contraceptive agent and appears in fixed‑dose combination hormone products marketed as Prempro (ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE), which contain conjugated estrogens 0.625 mg combined with either 2.5 mg or 5 mg of medroxyprogesterone acetate. These combination products belong to the broader class of progestogens and estrogens in combination therapy, and they likewise list polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive ingredient. Leuprolide, a member of the L [Preparations] family, is indicated for the treatment of uterine lining tumors. In contrast, levonorgestrel is contraindicated in patients with uterine lining tumors, highlighting a therapeutic overlap in the management of this condition. Overall, the knowledge base links synthetic progestins such as levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate to hormonal contraception, identifies the progesterone receptor as their shared pharmacologic target, details the active and inactive components of their formulations, and outlines specific clinical considerations, including contraindications related to uterine lining tumors.
